did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a multi-scale AI deep learning approach for skin cancer detection. Specifically, it employs transfer learning with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) initialized with pre-trained weights from the ImageNet dataset to classify melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions. The method includes image preprocessing, data augmentation, and combines outputs from three independently trained CNNs into a fully connected network (FCN). The study achieved 90.09% accuracy on the ISIC 2020 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a proposed multi-scale AI deep learning approach for skin cancer detection. It details specific methods (transfer learning with CNNs, image augmentation, fully connected networks), describes the dataset used (ISIC 2020), and reports original results (90.09% accuracy). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICAECT), it contains original technical contributions and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection Using A Multi-Scale AI Deep Learning Approach
journal: 2025 Fifth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communication and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a multi-scale AI deep learning approach for automated skin cancer detection, specifically distinguishing between melanoma and non-melanoma lesions. The methodology employs transfer learning with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) initialized using pre-trained ImageNet weights, combined with image preprocessing and data augmentation techniques to address class imbalance. The model aggregates outputs from three independently trained CNNs into a fully connected network to enhance feature extraction and classification accuracy. Evaluation was conducted on the ISIC 2020 dataset, which included 33,126 dermoscopic images, with approximately 1,400 reserved for testing. The proposed system achieved a testing accuracy of 90.09%, along with 90.01% recall and 92.34% precision. The results indicate that the multi-scale framework outperforms alternative approaches by focusing on suspicious central regions and improving diagnostic power through balanced data and augmentation.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). The paper states the method differentiates melanoma from benign tumours/non-melanoma lesions.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The paper notes the ISIC 2020 dataset contains nine skin condition categories. While the experiment focuses on melanoma vs. non-melanoma, the text references other classes present in related datasets or literature (e.g., SCC, BCC, SK, nevus) to contextualize the 'non-melanoma' group or discuss prior work. The specific composition of the 'non-melanoma' class in the ISIC 2020 subset used for the binary task is not explicitly enumerated beyond the general term.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma versus non-melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is to 'differentiate it from benign tumours' and 'classify melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions'. The model uses a SoftMax layer for 'two output classes' (binary classification). The study reports classification metrics including accuracy (90.09% or 95.13% in Table 1 depending on the specific experimental setup referenced, but consistently binary), recall (90.01%), and precision (92.34%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image collection from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2020 dataset, containing images from 2,000 individuals.

use.training: Used for training the multi-scale AI deep learning approach after balancing the dataset and applying image augmentation (rotating images by 10 degrees to create 35 permutations per image).
use.testing: Approximately 4% of the dataset (1,400 photos) was set aside for testing to evaluate the model's performance.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale hierarchical image database used for pre-training weights.

use.training: Used to initialize the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) weights via transfer learning before fine-tuning on the skin cancer dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance and derive metrics such as accuracy, recall, and precision.
To visualize the trade-off between sensitivity (true positive rate) and specificity (false positive rate) for the model.
To quantify the overall discriminative ability of the model across all classification thresholds.
To address class imbalance in the dataset by raising the representation of minority classes and decreasing majority classes, thereby reducing bias in model training.
To assign unique classification costs to each class to ensure minority groups receive proper priority during training.
To increase dataset size and mitigate overfitting by creating variants of existing images (e.g., rotating by 10 degrees to create 35 permutations).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, ROC curves, and confusion matrices, with comparisons made qualitatively or via metric values against other methods without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2020.notes: The proposed method was evaluated on the ISIC-2020 dataset. The results indicate that while the accuracy matches some alternative approaches, it performs better in terms of recall and precision. The study notes that the model concentrates more on malignant areas, providing higher diagnostic power. The dataset consisted of 33,126 dermoscopic images, with approximately 1,400 (4%) set aside for testing. The training set was augmented to 31,726 photos. The paper also mentions a testing accuracy of 90.09% in the abstract, but Table 1 reports 95.13% accuracy for the proposed approach on ISIC-2020.

ISIC-2016.notes: Results from related work using the ISIC 2016 dataset are cited for comparison. Ali and associates used LightNet for classification.

ISIC (Esteva et al.).notes: Results from Esteva et al. using CNNs on the ISIC dataset are cited. They performed two categorisation assignments: melanoma vs all non-melanoma lesions and melanoma vs atypical lesions.

3,600 Lesion Images (Sagar et al.).notes: Results from Sagar et al. using ResNet-50 on approximately 3,600 lesion images from the ISIC dataset are cited. The paper notes that deep learning models like ResNet-50 performed at least 11% better than other models in this context.

2,742 Lesion Images (Jojoa Acosta et al..notes: Results from Jojoa Acosta et al. using a mask and region-based CNN with ResNet152 on 2,742 ISIC photos are cited. The paper states this approach outperformed InceptionV3 and DenseNet169.

3,600 Lesion Images (Harangi et al.).notes: Results from Harangi et al. using an ensemble of deep neural networks (AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogleNet) on 3,600 lesion images are cited.

170-SKIN Lesion Images.notes: Results from Dorge et al. using deep support vector machines on 170-SKIN lesion images are cited. They achieved the greatest percentages of 95.1% (SCC), 98.9% (actinic keratosis), and 94.17% (SCC) in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for specific classes, with overall metrics listed in Table 1.

ISIC-2018 (Rashid et al.).notes: Results from Rashid et al. using GANs and the ISIC 2018 dataset for diagnosing vascular lesions, melanoma, melanocytic nevus, and benign keratosis are cited.

ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 (Bisla et al.).notes: Results from Bisla et al. using deep adaptive GANs on ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets are cited.

ISIC-2018 (Ali et al.).notes: Results from Ali et al. using a self-focusing PGAN on the ISIC 2018 dataset for identifying pigmented benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, nevus, vascular lesions, and pigmented Bowen's disease are cited. Accuracies of 19.7% and 70.1% were achieved under various situations.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-scale AI deep learning approach consisting of three independently trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) combined into a Fully Connected Network (FCN).
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning initialized with pre-trained weights from the ImageNet dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images from ISIC 2020 dataset used).

architecture_details.structure: Three independently trained CNNs whose outputs are aggregated and fed into a Fully Connected Network (FCN).
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Focuses on the central suspicious region of the image using level set-based segmentation and Kapur's Multi-Thresholding optimized with the bat method for preprocessing.
architecture_details.heads: Binary classification head for two output classes (melanoma vs. non-melanoma).

training_details.optimizer: Backpropagation used to fine-tune weights based on output error.
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (SoftMax layer used for classification).

hyperparameters.learning_rate: Modest learning rate used to avoid excessive weight volatility.
hyperparameters.epochs: 25 epochs mentioned in context of training accuracy stagnation.
hyperparameters.augmentation: Image augmentation rotating images by 10 degrees to create 35 permutations per image; dataset balancing via resampling.

training_details.training_schedule: Model trained first on original dataset without augmentation, then on balanced dataset with augmentation.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully Connected Network (FCN) aggregating outputs from three CNNs.
classification_or_output_head.final_layer: SoftMax layer swapped out for one specific to two output classes (binary classification).
classification_or_output_head.alternative: Sigmoid function mentioned as an alternative for binary classification.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Multi-Scale AI Deep Learning Approach (Three independently trained CNNs combined into a Fully Connected Network)
preprocessing_techniques: Image preprocessing to reduce lighting effects and noise
Kapur's Multi-Thresholding optimized with the bat method for image preprocessing
Level set-based segmentation to identify melanoma-affected regions (used in postprocessing/preprocessing context for feature extraction)
Image normalization by averaging each image's red, green, and blue (RGB) values
Dataset balancing (resampling) to address class imbalance
Image augmentation by rotating images by 10 degrees to create 35 permutations per image

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated by 10 degrees to create 35 permutations per image, increasing the training set from 1,400 to 31,726 photos.
Resampling techniques were used to address class imbalance by raising the representation of minority classes and decreasing the number of majority classes.
Unique classification costs were assigned to each class to ensure minority groups received proper priority during training.
Data augmentation approaches included the development of pseudo-samples to improve the model's capacity to identify characteristics of under-represented classes.

conclusions: The proposed multi-scale AI deep learning approach, which combines outputs from three independently trained CNNs into a fully connected network, achieved a testing accuracy of 90.09% on the ISIC 2020 dataset.
The method outperformed alternative approaches in terms of recall and precision, despite matching them in accuracy, due to its focus on the central suspicious region of skin lesions.
Dataset balancing and image augmentation (rotating images by 10 degrees to create 35 permutations) were critical for addressing class imbalance and improving model resilience and diagnostic power.
The model demonstrated good generalization, with the accuracy difference between training and validation datasets decreasing from 20% to 2-3% after applying drop layers and data augmentation.

Training the model on the original unbalanced dataset without augmentation yielded inadequate results, highlighting the necessity of dataset balancing for handling the unequal distribution of skin condition categories.

The study acknowledges that training accuracy remained between 70% and 75% after 25 epochs, suggesting the model had not yet learned all important characteristics, potentially due to the original data imbalance.

Future work aims to investigate cutting-edge methods for managing data imbalance, such as tailored data augmentation tactics, and to explore more sophisticated deep learning architectures and improved validation techniques.
important_remarks: The paper reports conflicting accuracy values for the proposed method on the ISIC 2020 dataset: 90.09% in the abstract and 95.13% in Table 1.
The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC 2020 dataset without testing on independent cohorts.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; comparisons with other methods are based on reported metric values rather than statistical significance.

The model was trained on a binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma), but the underlying ISIC 2020 dataset contains nine skin condition categories, potentially obscuring class-specific performance nuances.
The paper acknowledges that training accuracy stagnated between 70% and 75% after 25 epochs, suggesting the model may not have fully converged or learned all important characteristics.
Comparisons with prior works (e.g., Esteva et al., Harangi et al.) are limited because the authors note that 'various methodologies cannot have the same test sets,' preventing direct fair comparison.
The hardware specifications are vaguely described ('Lambda laptop, CPU (Intel Core i5)'), lacking details on GPU usage which is critical for deep learning reproducibility.
The text contains irrelevant content regarding 'brain tumor identification' and 'SVM/LBP' in the Results section, indicating potential copy-paste errors or lack of rigorous editing.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while a 4% hold-out test set is mentioned, the specific protocol for train/validation/test splits and augmentation application (whether applied before or after split) is not fully detailed to rule out leakage.
